architectureSummary.overview: PaymentReminder is a .NET 8 BackgroundService (IHostedService) that runs on a schedule, queries the SQL Server database for overdue invoices, and sends email reminders via SMTP. It's a single-process console application hosted as a Windows Service / systemd service.
architectureSummary.requestLifecycle: 1. Timer fires every hour → 2. BackgroundService.ExecuteAsync resumes → 3. Queries EF Core for unpaid invoices past due date → 4. Filters those not yet reminded in last 24h → 5. Builds email from template → 6. Sends via SmtpClient → 7. Updates reminder timestamp in DB → 8. Sleeps until next hour

questions.questionText: How did you implement the scheduled processing in the PaymentReminder service?
questions.answer:
The PaymentReminder service extends BackgroundService and runs in an infinite loop with Task.Delay between iterations:

public class PaymentReminderWorker : BackgroundService
{
    private readonly ILogger<PaymentReminderWorker> _logger;
    private readonly IServiceScopeFactory _scopeFactory;
    private readonly IConfiguration _config;

    public PaymentReminderWorker(
        ILogger<PaymentReminderWorker> logger,
        IServiceScopeFactory scopeFactory,
        IConfiguration config)
    {
        _logger = logger;
        _scopeFactory = scopeFactory;
        _config = config;
    }

    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("PaymentReminder started");

        while (!stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            try
            {
                await ProcessRemindersAsync(stoppingToken);
            }
            catch (Exception ex) when (ex is not OperationCanceledException)
            {
                _logger.LogError(ex, "Error processing reminders — will retry next interval");
            }

            // Wait until next run (configurable, default 1 hour)
            var interval = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(
                _config.GetValue<int>("Reminder:IntervalMinutes", 60));

            await Task.Delay(interval, stoppingToken);  // CT cancels delay on shutdown
        }
    }
}

Why IServiceScopeFactory: BackgroundService is a Singleton, but EF Core's DbContext is Scoped. I create a new scope per processing run to get a fresh DbContext — prevents the "using a disposed DbContext" error.
followUpQuestions: Why is DbContext Scoped, not Singleton?
How do you handle the service crashing mid-send?
questions.importantForInterview: IServiceScopeFactory pattern is a critical .NET interview topic. Singleton services cannot directly inject Scoped services — you must create a scope and resolve from it. EF Core's DbContext is explicitly designed as Scoped.

questions.questionText: How did you prevent duplicate reminders from being sent?
questions.answer:
Preventing duplicate sends was critical — double-reminding a client is a bad experience. I solved it with two complementary mechanisms:

1. DB-level idempotency:

public class ReminderLog
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public int InvoiceId { get; set; }
    public DateTime SentAt { get; set; }
    public string RecipientEmail { get; set; } = string.Empty;
}

// Unique constraint: can only log one reminder per invoice per day
public void Configure(EntityTypeBuilder<ReminderLog> builder)
{
    builder.HasIndex(r => new { r.InvoiceId, r.SentDate })
           .IsUnique();
}

2. Transactional send-and-log:

private async Task SendAndLogAsync(Invoice invoice, CancellationToken ct)
{
    await using var transaction = await _db.Database.BeginTransactionAsync(ct);
    try
    {
        // Check not already sent today
        var alreadySent = await _db.ReminderLogs
            .AnyAsync(r => r.InvoiceId == invoice.Id &&
                          r.SentAt.Date == DateTime.UtcNow.Date, ct);
        if (alreadySent) return;

        // Send email (outside transaction — cannot rollback SMTP)
        await _emailSender.SendAsync(invoice.ClientEmail, BuildEmailBody(invoice), ct);

        // Log the send within the transaction
        _db.ReminderLogs.Add(new ReminderLog
        {
            InvoiceId = invoice.Id,
            SentAt = DateTime.UtcNow,
            RecipientEmail = invoice.ClientEmail
        });
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
        await transaction.CommitAsync(ct);
    }
    catch
    {
        await transaction.RollbackAsync(ct);
        throw;
    }
}

Limitation I acknowledged: SMTP send and DB log aren't truly atomic. If the send succeeds but DB write fails, the next run will resend. The unique constraint on InvoiceId+Date reduces (not eliminates) this window.
followUpQuestions: How would you make this truly idempotent?
What is the outbox pattern and would it help here?
questions.importantForInterview: Acknowledging the limitation shows senior-level thinking. The interviewer expects you to know that SMTP and DB aren't atomically linked — the correct solution is the Outbox pattern (write reminder intent to DB in same transaction, separate publisher sends and marks complete).
